summaries.Economics: The main elements of the FSM economy vary from state to state but include subsistence farming, local fishing, lease of fishing rights to FSM waters, servicing of fishing fleets, government employment, and tourism. Tourism is considered to have potential for near-term income generation due to the natural and marine beauty of the islands, though limitations in air transportation, land-use issues, and competition with other island destinations closer to tourist markets represent challenges to developing larger-scale tourism investment. The FSM considers the ocean to be the country's most important resource, earning in recent years $18-24 million annually in licensing fees paid by foreign vessels for tuna fishing in the FSM's Exclusive Economic Zone. Rising sea levels are identified as a threat to infrastructure and local economies across Pacific Island regions, exacerbating existing inequities. Socioeconomic data gaps persist across Pacific Island jurisdictions, stemming from ongoing exclusion in data collection efforts that perpetuate historical social injustices reinforced by colonial and postcolonial governance systems.
summaries.Time zone: Howland and Baker Islands operate on a UTC-12:00 offset, designated as the Y (Yankee) nautical time zone, making them among the last places on Earth to experience any given calendar date. This UTC-12:00 designation places them in the IDLW (International Date Line West) time zone, on the Western Hemisphere side of the International Date Line. As a result, for any given date, the latest place on Earth where that date would still be valid is on Howland and Baker Islands, which is the basis for the "Anywhere on Earth" (AoE) calendar designation used to specify deadlines without requiring timezone calculations.
summaries.History: Howland Island was first sighted in 1822 by George Worth, a Nantucket whaling-ship captain, who named it Worth Island, and in 1842 another American whaling ship visited the island, renaming it Howland Island after that ship's lookout. In 1791, Edmund Fanning, an American sailor, is also noted as having discovered the island, marking its entrance into recorded history. The United States claimed Howland in 1857, along with nearby Baker Island, under the Guano Act of 1856. Prior to 1890, organic nitrate (guano) was mined from the island by both the United States and the British, though the atoll's guano deposits are now exhausted. The island was colonized from Hawaii in 1935. In 1937, famous pilot Amelia Earhart attempted to land there during her around-the-world flight but never made it, adding an enduring mystery to the island's story. During World War II, the island served as a lookout point for the U.S. military. Today, it is part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system and the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, with no permanent residents and very limited human presence.
summaries.National Wildlife Refuge: Howland Island, a coral atoll and unincorporated territory of the United States located in the southwestern Pacific Ocean approximately 1,650 miles southwest of Honolulu, is currently part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge system, providing nesting areas and forage for a variety of birds and marine wildlife, including several species of migratory seabirds and shorebirds as well as threatened and endangered sea turtles. The island was designated a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and was also designated part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument in 2009, which helps safeguard marine resources in the area. The island has no permanent inhabitants and serves an essential role for science and ecology, with researchers visiting to study its wildlife and environment, helping to protect coral reefs and unique bird species. The island is composed of coral fragments and is surrounded by an active fringing reef, and high-resolution images captured by astronauts aboard the International Space Station are used to update geographic maps of reefs and islands, assess the health of reef ecosystems, and calculate bathymetry of the surrounding ocean bottom.
summaries.Earhart Light: The Earhart Light is a small lighthouse built in 1937 on Howland Island, a 450-acre uninhabited coral island located in an extremely remote part of the Pacific Ocean, almost halfway between Hawaii and Australia. It was constructed in anticipation of Amelia Earhart's scheduled refueling stop on the island during her 1937 transpacific flight, though Earhart never reached the island and is thought to have crashed in the waters just north of Howland Island. During World War II, on January 31st, 1941, a Japanese submarine fired 50 shells into the settlement on Howland Island, rendering the Amelia Earhart light beacon useless, and the station has been inactive since 1942, with the structure now considered a "day beacon." Brief visits have since been made to the island to restore the light beacon and to add the names of two colonists lost during the Japanese attack. The beacon remains on Howland Island today, which has been a National Wildlife Refuge under the jurisdiction of the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service since 1974, and the island has remained uninhabited by humans except for brief scientific visits since the remaining colonists were evacuated in 1941.
summaries.Image gallery: The gallery of Howland Island includes a map of Howland Island and an image titled ISS010-E-09287, photographed on 3 December 2004 by an Expedition 10 crewmember aboard the International Space Station (ISS), showing a view of Howland Island from an altitude of 192 nautical miles (356 km) at a look angle of 41 degrees facing southwest, captured using a Kodak DCS760c Electronic Still Camera with an 800 mm focal length, sourced from the JSC Gateway to Astronaut Photography of Earth and credited to the Earth Science and Remote Sensing Unit at Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center, with approximately 10% cloud coverage, a sun azimuth of 115°, and a sun elevation of 19°, depicting the island's coral fragment composition and surrounding active fringing reef visible through white breakers encircling the island.
